plainMeaning: Here the reciter confesses any wrong attitude toward the final death-fast, then sums up that of the eighty-five possible slips against the twelve vows whatever he committed — knowingly or not, great or tiny — may it all come to nothing, and finally reviews his conduct in knowledge, faith, austerity and effort.
recitation: apacchima maraṇāntika saṁleṣaṇātaṇā pāṁca atiyāra śodhuṁ. ihalogā saṁsappaoge, paralogā saṁsappaoge, jiviyā saṁsappaoge, maraṇā saṁsappaoge, kāmabhogā saṁsappaoge, ihaloka āśrī ṛddhi, vṛddhitaṇī āśaṁsā vāṁchā kīdhī hoya; paraloka āśrī iṁdra, cakravartītaṇī āśaṁsā vāṁchā kīdhī hoya; jivitavya ane maraṇātaṇī āśaṁsā vāṁchā kīdhī hoya; kāma bhogataṇī āśaṁsā vāṁchā kīdhī hoya. aneruṁ e saṁleṣaṇā viṣe. ॥ evaṁkāre śrī samyaktvamūla bāra vrata viṣe paṁcyāśī atiyāramāṁhe jiko koi atiyāra, anācāra, atikrama, vyatikrama huo hoya, tathā jāṇatāṁ ajāṇatāṁ sūkṣma, bādara, kāno, mātrā, miṁḍī, pada, akṣara, ochho, adhiko, haḷavo, bhārī, āgala, pāchala, kahyo kahevāṇo hoya; te savi huṁ mane, vacane, kāyāe karī micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ. 1 tathā jñānācāre jñāna rahe āśātanā kīdhī hoya. paḍhatāṁ, guṇatāṁ aṁtarāya kīdho hoya. macchara dharyo hoya. akṣarane thūṁka lagāḍyuṁ hoya. pāṭī, pothī, kavalī, ṭhavaṇī, nokāravālī, kāgale paga lagāḍyo hoya. khamāsamaṇa pākhe parivāḍī līdhī hoya. jñānadravyataṇī sārasaṁbhāḷa kīdhī na hoya. aneruṁ e jñānācāra viṣe. ॥ 1 ॥ 2. tathā darśanācāre samyaktva virādhiuṁ hoya. deva, guru namaskāra karyā pākhe bhojana kīdhuṁ hoya. deva pūjatāṁ dhotī śuddha kīdhī na hoya. mukhakośa kīdho na hoya. ādhaḍaviche deva pūjyā hoya. gurune āsaṇe, besaṇe, upagaraṇe paga lagāḍyo hoya; guruvacana dveṣalage vālyuṁ hoya. biṁba hāthathakī vichuṭyuṁ hoya, biṁbanuṁ vecāvacca kīdhuṁ na hoya, devadravya āpaṇe vyavasāye ghālyuṁ hoya. devadravya viṇasatuṁ uvekhyuṁ hoya. aneruṁ e darśanācāra viṣe. ॥ 2 ॥ 3. tapācāre chatī śakte pacchakkhāṇa kīdhuṁ na hoya, karīne bhāṁgyuṁ hoya, pacchakkhāṇa pālyuṁ na hoya, niyama, abhigraha lei bhāṁgyā hoya, cha bheda abhyaṁtara, cha bheda bāhya tapa avasare sācavyuṁ na hoya. aneruṁ e tapācāra viṣe. ॥ 3 ॥ 4. vīryācāre chatī śakte cha āvaśyaka samyak sācavyā na hoya. chatī veḷāe posaha sāmāyika kīdhuṁ na hoya. beṭhāṁ vāṁdaṇāṁ dīdhāṁ hoya. kāussagga adhikā adhiko vadhāryo na hoya. chatī śakte tapa kīdhuṁ na hoya. dveṣa karato koi vāryo na hoya. navuṁ paḍhatāṁ, āgaluṁ gaṇatāṁ, artha vicāra pūchatāṁ kahetāṁ āḷasa kīdhuṁ hoya. chatī sāmagrīe dāna dīdhuṁ na hoya. devayātrā guruyātrā kīdhī na hoya. sahāmmītaṇī bhakti sācavī na hoya. dharmavaṁtanī āpadā bhāṁgī na hoya. aneruṁ e vīryācāra viṣe. ॥ 4 ॥ aneruṁ je kāṁi jinavacana viruddha mana, vacana kāyāe karaṇa, karāvaṇa, anumatie karī samācaryuṁ hoya. te savi huṁ mane, vacane, kāyāe karī micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ ॥ paḍisiddhāṇaṁ karaṇe, kiccāṇamakaraṇe paḍikkamaṇaṁ. asaddahaṇe ya tahā, vivarīya parūvaṇāe ya ॥ 1 ॥ desāvagāsiaṁ, uvabhogaṁ paribhogaṁ pacchakkhāmi. annatthaṇābhogeṇaṁ, sahassāgāreṇaṁ, mahattarāgāreṇaṁ savvasamāhivattiyāgāreṇaṁ vosirāmi ॥

wordByWord.gloss: the last, final (the very last one)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit apacchima < Skt. apaścima 'not having a later one', i.e. final; epithet of the death-fast.
wordByWord.notes: Same root used of Mahāvīra as the apaścima (last) Tīrthaṅkara.

wordByWord.gloss: occurring at the end of life, death-related
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. maraṇa 'death' + antika 'reaching the end of', so 'lasting until death'.

wordByWord.gloss: saṁlekhanā (the voluntary final fast undertaken with equanimity)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. saṁlekhanā 'a scouring-thin' of body and passions; book spells it with ષ (saṁleṣaṇā).
wordByWord.notes: Defined in the preface glossary.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati genitive suffix -taṇā/-taṇī, from Apabhraṁśa -tanaa.
wordByWord.notes: Recurs as -taṇī, -taṇā throughout.
Glossary term.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. śudh 'to purify'; 1st-person present, the opening purification-formula of every block.
wordByWord.notes: The recurring 'pāñca atiyāra śodhuṁ' formula noted in the preface.
First saṁlekhanā atichāra.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit; reflects Skt. āśaṁsā-prayoga 'the employing of a wish/expectation' (also read as saṁ-sarpa 'creeping attachment').
wordByWord.notes: Locative; the Gujarati gloss that follows expands it as 'āśaṁsā vāṁchā' (wish, longing).
Second saṁlekhanā atichāra.
Third atichāra: craving to live on.
Fourth atichāra: wishing for a quick death.
Fifth atichāra.
Object of the this-worldly craving.
Paired with ṛddhi: 'wealth and growth'.
The technical fault-word: a covetous wish that pollutes the fast.
Near-synonym paired with āśaṁsā for emphasis.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati past participle of karavuṁ 'to do' (Skt. kṛta).
wordByWord.notes: Inflects kīdhī/kīdho/kīdhuṁ by gender of the object.

wordByWord.gloss: may have been, should it have happened
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. bhavati 'to be'; subjunctive 'if it be'.
wordByWord.notes: The confessional 'kīdhī hoya' = 'if I have done (it)'.
Emblem of supreme other-worldly status craved as a fault.
Highest human rank; coveting rebirth as such is the para-loka fault.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. jīvitavya 'that which is to be lived'.
wordByWord.notes: Restates the third atichāra in Gujarati.
Opens the catch-all closing formula of each block.
Closes 'aneruṁ e … viṣe' = 'and whatever else regarding this …'.
Opens the summary tally.
Reverential prefix.
The twelve vows stand on samyaktva — the arc the preface describes.
The bāra vrata: 5 aṇuvrata + 3 guṇavrata + 4 śikṣāvrata.
The total atichāra count tallied within this verse (see notes for the breakdown).
Glossary term; graver than atichāra.
First of the four-stage gradation of fault.
Second stage; atikrama → vyatikrama → atichāra → anācāra is the classic escalation.

wordByWord.etymology: from jāṇavuṁ 'to know' (Skt. jñā); the paired present participles.
wordByWord.notes: The exhaustiveness pair flagged in the preface.
Paired with bādara.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati orthographic terms: kāno (ā-stroke), mātrā (vowel mark), miṁḍī (the ṁ dot).
wordByWord.notes: Errors of recitation/writing at the level of single marks.
Reciting less or more than the text.
Faults of intonation/weight in chanting.

wordByWord.gloss: out of order — before or after
wordByWord.notes: Mis-sequencing the text.

wordByWord.gloss: spoken or caused to be spoken (mis-recited)
wordByWord.notes: Active and passive: said wrongly oneself or had it said wrongly.

wordByWord.gloss: I, by mind, speech and body
wordByWord.etymology: mana 'mind', vacana 'speech', kāya 'body' (Skt.); the threefold instrument of action.
wordByWord.notes: The trividha-karaṇa refrain closing each confession.

wordByWord.gloss: may that misdeed of mine be void
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit micchā 'false/void' + me 'my' + dukkaḍa (Skt. duṣkṛta) 'ill-done deed'.
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term; the formal release.
First of the pañcācāra reviewed here.
Here, disrespect shown toward knowledge/scripture.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit paḍh- 'read' (Skt. paṭh); guṇ- 'tell over, repeat'.

wordByWord.notes: Obstructing another's study.
Touching letters with spit-moistened fingers — disrespect to scripture.

wordByWord.token: પાટી, પોથી, કવલી, ઠવણી, નોકારવાલી, કાગલે
wordByWord.translit: pāṭī, pothī, kavalī, ṭhavaṇī, nokāravālī, kāgale
wordByWord.gloss: writing-board, manuscript, scripture-wrapper, book-stand, rosary, paper
wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati terms for study/worship implements; ṭhavaṇī = the wooden book-rest, nokāravālī = the 108-bead Navkar rosary.
wordByWord.notes: Touching these sacred articles with the foot is the fault.

wordByWord.gloss: set the foot to / touched with the foot

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit khamāsamaṇa 'O forbearing ascetic' (the prostration-salute); pākhe 'without'.

wordByWord.notes: Taking up the recitation-round without the due prostration.
Parallel to devadravya below.
Failing to maintain jñāna-endowment is the fault.

wordByWord.gloss: in the conduct concerning right faith
wordByWord.notes: Second ācāra.
Damaging one's samyaktva.
Worshipping without a ritually clean garment.

wordByWord.gloss: mouth-cloth (the cloth covering nose-and-mouth during pūjā)

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati idiom 'in a slapdash way'.
wordByWord.notes: Reading uncertain; rendered by sense — flagged in notes.

wordByWord.gloss: the guru's seat, sitting-mat, and implements
wordByWord.notes: Touching these with the foot is the fault.

wordByWord.gloss: turned aside the guru's word out of spite
wordByWord.notes: Rejecting the guru's instruction in ill-will.
Letting the image fall — irreverence.
Failing to render due care to the image.
Misappropriating temple-funds is a grave darśana-fault.

wordByWord.etymology: āpaṇe 'one's own'; vyavasāya 'trade'; ghālvuṁ 'to put in'.
wordByWord.notes: Diverting devadravya to private enterprise.

wordByWord.etymology: viṇas- (Skt. vinaś) 'to perish'; uvekh- (Skt. upekṣ) 'to overlook'.
wordByWord.notes: Negligently letting sacred property be lost.
Third ācāra.
Recurs as the standard rebuke: failing 'though able'.
Taking then breaking a vow.

wordByWord.token: છ ભેદ અભ્યંતર, છ ભેદ બાહ્ય
wordByWord.translit: cha bheda abhyaṁtara, cha bheda bāhya
wordByWord.gloss: the six inner and six outer kinds (of austerity)
wordByWord.notes: The classic twelvefold tapas (6 bāhya + 6 abhyantara).
Fourth ācāra reviewed; cāritrācāra is folded into the vow-by-vow body above.
The six Āvaśyakas that frame pratikramaṇa itself.

wordByWord.gloss: at the (proper) time, pauṣadha and sāmāyika
wordByWord.etymology: veḷā 'time'; posaha (Skt. pauṣadha) the day-retreat; sāmāyika the equanimity-vow.
beṭhāṁ 'seated'; vāṁdaṇāṁ (Skt. vandanaka) the veneration; dīdhāṁ 'gave'.
wordByWord.notes: Performing it improperly seated rather than with the full gestures.

wordByWord.gloss: did not lengthen the kāyotsarga more and more
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit kāussagga < Skt. kāyotsarga 'abandonment of the body'; vadhārvuṁ 'to increase'.
wordByWord.notes: Failing to extend the standing meditation as one is able.
Sloth in learning, revising, or explaining meaning.

wordByWord.gloss: charity, though the means were present
wordByWord.notes: Withholding charity despite ability.

wordByWord.gloss: pilgrimage to the Jina and attendance on the guru

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sāhammī < Skt. sādharmika 'one of the same faith'; bhakti 'service'.

wordByWord.gloss: relieved the distress of the pious
wordByWord.etymology: dharmavant 'virtuous one'; āpadā 'calamity'; bhāṁgvuṁ here 'to break/remove'.
wordByWord.notes: Failing to relieve a devout person's hardship.

wordByWord.etymology: jina-vacana 'the teaching of the Jina'; viruddha 'opposed'.
wordByWord.notes: The universal sweep-up clause.

wordByWord.gloss: by doing, by causing to be done, and by consenting
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. karaṇa / kāraṇa / anumati — the threefold mode of agency.
wordByWord.notes: With mind-speech-body these give the ninefold scope of culpability.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit paḍisiddha < Skt. pratiṣiddha 'prohibited'; karaṇe 'in the doing'.
wordByWord.notes: Line 1 of the closing Prakrit gāthā.
Sandhi of kiccāṇaṁ + akaraṇe.
Glossary term; the act itself names the remedy.

wordByWord.gloss: and likewise for disbelief (in true doctrine)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit a-saddahaṇa < Skt. a-śraddhāna 'lack of faith'; ya 'and'; tahā 'so, likewise'.
Prakrit vivarīya < Skt. viparīta 'reversed'; parūvaṇā < prarūpaṇā 'exposition'.
wordByWord.notes: The four objects of pratikramaṇa: doing the forbidden, omitting the obligatory, disbelief, and mis-teaching.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Skt. deśāvakāśika 'limited to a region', the 10th lay vow.
wordByWord.notes: Opens the concluding pratyākhyāna.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit uvabhoga/paribhoga < Skt. upabhoga (consumables, e.g. food) / paribhoga (re-usables, e.g. clothes).
Prakrit < Skt. pratyākhyāmi 'I formally give up'.
wordByWord.notes: 1st-person of the vow.

wordByWord.gloss: save by inadvertence (the exception of forgetfulness)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit annattha 'otherwise/except' + aṇābhoga 'absence of attention' (Skt. anābhoga).
wordByWord.notes: First of the standard āgāras (exceptions that do not break the vow).
Second āgāra.
Third āgāra: a graver duty supervenes.

wordByWord.gloss: and for the sake of complete (mental) equanimity
wordByWord.etymology: sabba 'all' + samāhi (Skt. samādhi) 'composure' + pattiya 'on account of' + āgāra.
wordByWord.notes: Fourth āgāra: action permitted to preserve total samādhi.
The terminal renunciation-word sealing the vow and the whole confession.

literalTranslation: I cleanse the five transgressions of the final, death-bound saṁlekhanā: craving applied to this world, to the next world, to (continued) life, to death, to sensual enjoyments. If, regarding this world, I have made a wish or longing for wealth and growth; if, regarding the next world, I have made a wish for (the state of) Indra or a universal emperor; if I have made a wish for living or for death; if I have made a longing for sensual enjoyment — and whatever else regarding the saṁlekhanā. ‖ Thus, regarding the twelve vows rooted in right faith, among the eighty-five transgressions, whatever transgression, breach, first-overstep or further-overstep has occurred, and whether knowingly or unknowingly — subtle or gross, in vowel-stroke, vowel-sign, dot, word or syllable, too little or too much, light or heavy, before or after, mis-spoken or mis-caused-to-be-spoken — all that I, by mind, speech and body, declare: may my misdeed be void. (1) Likewise, in the conduct of knowledge: if I have shown disrespect where knowledge abides, caused obstruction to one reading or reciting, harboured envy, touched letters with spittle, set my foot to slate, manuscript, scripture-wrapper, book-rest, rosary or paper, taken up the recitation-round without the prostration, or failed to maintain the endowment for knowledge — and whatever else regarding the conduct of knowledge. ‖1‖ (2) Likewise, in the conduct of faith: if I have violated right faith; eaten without saluting deva and guru; not purified the worship-cloth while worshipping; not worn the mouth-cloth; worshipped carelessly; set my foot to the guru's seat, mat or implements; rejected the guru's word in spite; let the image slip from my hand; not rendered service to the image; put temple-property into my own business or overlooked its being wasted — and whatever else regarding the conduct of faith. ‖2‖ (3) In the conduct of austerity: if, though able, I have not made a renunciatory vow, or having made it broke it, or did not keep it, or took rule and resolve and broke them, or failed to observe the six inner and six outer austerities at their season — and whatever else regarding austerity. ‖3‖ (4) In the conduct of energy: if, though able, I have not rightly kept the six obligatory acts; not performed pauṣadha and sāmāyika in their time; given the vandana seated; not lengthened the kāyotsarga as I could; not done austerity though able; not restrained one acting in malice; been slothful in learning anew, revising, or asking and explaining meaning; not given charity though the means were present; not made pilgrimage to the Jina or to the guru; not kept up devotion to co-religionists; not relieved the distress of the pious — and whatever else regarding the conduct of energy. ‖4‖ And whatever else, contrary to the Jina's word, I have done, caused to be done, or consented to, by mind, speech or body — all that I, by mind, speech and body, declare: may my misdeed be void. ‖ For doing the forbidden, for not doing the obligatory, there is pratikramaṇa; and likewise for disbelief and for contrary teaching. (1) I renounce the deśāvagāśika (limiting) of consumables and re-usables; save by inadvertence, by a sudden act, by an overriding necessity, and for the sake of complete equanimity — I cast (the rest) off.
idiomaticTranslation: Now I scour clean the five faults that can stain the final fast: a craving turned toward this world, toward the next, toward staying alive, toward dying, or toward pleasure. Should I have hankered, for this life, after wealth and increase; or coveted, for the next, the throne of Indra or of a world-emperor; or wished either to live on or to die; or yearned for sensual delight — and anything else amiss in my saṁlekhanā — I own it. So too, across the twelve vows that rest upon right faith, among their eighty-five possible lapses — whatever breach or overstep I made, knowing or unknowing, in matters subtle or gross, in a single vowel-stroke or dot, a word or a syllable, saying too little or too much, too softly or too heavily, out of order, mis-spoken by me or through me — all of it, by mind, speech and body: may that misdeed of mine come to nothing. I review, too, my fourfold conduct. In knowledge: irreverence to learning, blocking another's study, envy, soiling the letters with spittle, letting my foot touch slate, book, wrapper, stand, rosary or paper, taking up recitation without due prostration, neglecting the endowment of learning. In faith: wounding my right faith, eating before saluting the Lord and guru, an impure worship-cloth, no mouth-cloth, careless worship, my foot against the guru's seat, mat or things, spurning his word in spite, the icon slipped from my hand, service to it left undone, temple-funds turned to private trade or let go to ruin. In austerity: leaving a vow untaken though able, taken and broken, unkept, a rule or resolve abandoned, the twelve austerities unobserved in their season. In energy: the six obligatories slackly kept, pauṣadha and sāmāyika let pass, vandana given while seated, kāyotsarga not lengthened, austerity shirked, a malicious act unchecked, sloth in study and teaching, charity withheld though I could give, pilgrimage to Jina and guru omitted, service to fellow-believers and relief of the pious left undone. And whatever else I did against the Jina's word — by my own hand, through another, or by my mere consent, in thought, word or deed — may that misdeed of mine come to nothing. As the gāthā teaches: pratikramaṇa is owed for doing the forbidden, for leaving the duty undone, for wrong belief, and for false teaching. And so I take up the day's space-limiting vow: I renounce all but my measured use of consumable and re-usable things — excepting only inadvertence, sudden mischance, an overriding duty, and what the keeping of full inner calm requires — and the rest I cast away.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This closing verse performs three movements in one breath, and its placement is deliberate: only after the twelve vows (verse 12 ended with the twelfth, atithi-saṁvibhāga) does the reciter turn to the very last vow a householder can take — saṁlekhanā, the equanimous fast at death. Its five atichāras are not failures of fasting but failures of attitude: the fast is spoilt the moment it is bent toward a wish — to live (jiviyā), to die (maraṇā), to enjoy (kāmabhogā), or to be rewarded here (ihalogā: ṛddhi-vṛddhi) or hereafter (paralogā: the rank of Indra or a cakravartin). The Gujarati gloss makes the abstract Prakrit concrete: the danger is not death but desire smuggled into death. Note how the verse names the two extreme cravings — clinging to life and courting death — as equal faults; equanimity (samādhi) is the narrow path between them, which is exactly why the verse's final pratyākhyāna ends on savva-samāhi-vattiyā ('for the sake of complete equanimity'). The middle movement is the great tally: 'paṁcyāśī atiyāra' — eighty-five transgressions — closing the samyaktva-to-saṁlekhanā arc and then dissolving even the act of confession into its smallest possible units (kāno, mātrā, miṁḍī — a stroke, a sign, a dot), so that no error of recitation can hide. The third movement pivots from what is confessed to how one confesses well: a rapid audit of four of the five ācāras — jñāna, darśana, tapa, vīrya (cāritra having been examined vow-by-vow throughout). The recurring whip is 'chatī śakte' — 'though you had the power' — locating fault not in inability but in negligence. The verse then seals itself with the Prakrit gāthā 'paḍisiddhāṇaṁ karaṇe…', which compresses the whole logic of pratikramaṇa into four triggers — doing the forbidden, omitting the obligatory, disbelief, false teaching — and finally with the deśāvagāśika pratyākhyāna, whose terminal 'vosirāmi' ('I cast off') is the perfect last word for a confession that has, item by item, been letting go.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: Two doctrinal structures anchor the verse. First, the five atichāras of saṁlekhanā: this Āvaśyaka recension lists them by craving-object (iha-loka, para-loka, jīvita, maraṇa, kāma-bhoga), which maps onto — but is verbally distinct from — the Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.37 set (jīvitāśaṁsā, maraṇāśaṁsā, mitrānurāga, sukhānubandha, nidāna). Both traditions agree on the principle: even a fast unto death is voided by āśaṁsā, expectant desire, because the soul's release depends on detachment, not on the dying. Second, the pañcācāra: right conduct is fivefold — jñānācāra, darśanācāra, cāritrācāra, tapācāra, vīryācāra — and the verse audits four of them explicitly (cāritra is the substance of the whole vow-by-vow body that precedes). Vīryācāra is doctrinally pivotal: vīrya (spiritual energy) is what makes the other four operative, so its faults are all faults of withholding effort one possessed — hence the refrain chatī śakte. The closing gāthā encodes the Jain definition of pratikramaṇa itself as fourfold liability (commission, omission, mis-belief, mis-teaching), and the deśāvagāśika pratyākhyāna with its four āgāras shows the characteristic Jain realism: a vow is taken with built-in exceptions (anābhoga, sahasā, mahattara, samādhi-pratyaya) so that it binds the will without becoming a fresh source of guilt.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For today's reciter the verse is a lesson in motive-checking. The saṁlekhanā atichāras translate, well before death, into a daily diagnostic: am I doing this practice for a reward here, a reward hereafter, to escape life, or to cling to it? Any 'yes' is the fault. The eighty-five tally and the kāno-mātrā-miṁḍī clause teach scrupulous honesty about small things — including careless recitation — without scrupulosity's despair, since each block ends in release. The pañcācāra audit, governed by chatī śakte, asks the modern practitioner the sharpest question: not 'could I?' but 'I could, and did I?' — converting vague guilt into a concrete checklist (study, reverence to texts and teachers, austerity in season, the six obligatories, charity, pilgrimage, service to co-religionists). The final vosirāmi models the right closing gesture: name the lapse, then let it go.

notes: This is an unusually long composite verse: it bundles the saṁlekhanā atichāras, the eighty-five-atichāra summary tally, the four-ācāra review (jñāna/darśana/tapa/vīrya), the Prakrit gāthā 'paḍisiddhāṇaṁ karaṇe…', and the closing deśāvagāśika pratyākhyāna. Word-by-word coverage groups the verse's many repeated formulaic phrases (kīdhī/kīdho/kīdhuṁ hoya; aneruṁ e … viṣe; the micchā-mi-dukkaḍaṁ refrain; cha … the vow-numbering ‖N‖) under representative entries rather than re-glossing each recurrence, while every distinct lexical item is glossed. VARIANT: the five saṁlekhanā atichāras here are named by craving-object (ihaloga/paraloga/jiviya/maraṇa/kāmabhoga + āśaṁsā), which is the Āvaśyaka/śrāvaka recension; the Tattvārtha 7.37 list (jīvitāśaṁsā, maraṇāśaṁsā, mitrānurāga, sukhānubandha, nidāna) differs verbally — both are noted, neither conflated. 'saṁsappaoge' is read as āśaṁsā-prayoga ('application of a wish'), consistent with the verse's own Gujarati gloss 'āśaṁsā vāṁchā'; an alternative derivation from saṁ-sarpa ('creeping' attachment) is possible and is flagged at the token. 'ādhaḍaviche' is an obscure Old-Gujarati idiom rendered by sense ('carelessly/half-heartedly'); reading uncertain. The eighty-five total (paṁcyāśī) is stated by the text itself and corroborated by the standard breakdown given in the preface notes (5 samyaktva + 25 for the five aṇuvratas + 5 diśi + 5 bhogopabhoga-bhojana + 15 karmādāna + 5 anartha-daṇḍa + 5 sāmāyika + 5 deśāvagāśika + 5 pauṣadha + 5 saṁlekhanā = 85). Orthography (saṁleṣaṇā with ષ, pacchakkhāṇa/pacchakkhāmi variants, kāussagga) is preserved verbatim per the book's fidelity rule and not normalised.
